idioms.name: use_smart_pointers
idioms.description: Use std::unique_ptr for sole ownership and std::shared_ptr only when ownership is genuinely shared. Never use raw new/delete.
idioms.detection: Detect raw new/delete expressions, raw pointer declarations for owned resources.
idioms.fix: Replace with std::make_unique<T>() or std::make_shared<T>(). Use raw pointers only for non-owning observation.
idioms.bad_example: Widget* w = new Widget(); ... delete w;

idioms.name: use_raii
idioms.description: Use RAII for all resource management — files, locks, sockets, memory. Destructors should release resources.
idioms.detection: Detect manual resource acquire/release pairs without RAII wrappers. Detect bare mutex lock/unlock.
idioms.fix: Wrap resources in RAII types. Use std::lock_guard or std::scoped_lock for mutexes.

idioms.name: const_correctness
idioms.description: Mark variables, parameters, and methods const wherever possible. Use constexpr for compile-time constants.
idioms.detection: Detect non-const references/pointers that are never modified. Detect methods that don't modify state but lack const qualifier.
idioms.fix: Add const to all variables that don't change. Add const to method signatures. Use constexpr for compile-time values.

idioms.name: prefer_algorithms
idioms.description: Prefer STL algorithms and ranges over raw loops. std::find, std::transform, std::accumulate express intent more clearly.
idioms.detection: Detect for/while loops that implement standard algorithm patterns (search, transform, accumulate, count, filter).
idioms.fix: Replace manual loops with appropriate STL algorithms or C++20 ranges.
idioms.bad_example: int sum = 0; for (int i = 0; i < v.size(); i++) sum += v[i];

idioms.name: move_semantics
idioms.description: Use move semantics for expensive-to-copy objects. Accept by value and move when the callee needs ownership.
idioms.detection: Detect large object copies where moves would suffice. Detect missing std::move on rvalue-eligible transfers.
idioms.fix: Use std::move when transferring ownership. Implement move constructors/assignment for resource-owning classes.
idioms.bad_example: void setName(const std::string& n) { name_ = n; }

idioms.name: use_string_view
idioms.description: Use std::string_view for read-only string parameters instead of const std::string&.
idioms.detection: Detect const std::string& parameters that are only read, never stored.
idioms.fix: Change parameter type to std::string_view. Only use const std::string& if the function stores the string.
idioms.bad_example: bool startsWith(const std::string& str, const std::string& prefix)

idioms.name: use_auto_judiciously
idioms.description: Use auto when the type is obvious from context (iterators, make_unique, casts). Avoid auto when it obscures the type.
idioms.detection: Detect explicit type declarations for iterator types and factory returns. Also detect auto where type is completely non-obvious.
idioms.fix: Use auto for iterators, smart pointer factories, and casts. Spell out types when clarity matters.

idioms.name: c_style_casts
idioms.description: C-style casts are unchecked and dangerous. Use static_cast, dynamic_cast, const_cast, or reinterpret_cast for explicit intent.

idioms.name: using_namespace_std
idioms.description: 'using namespace std;' pollutes the global namespace and can cause name collisions. Use explicit std:: prefix or targeted using declarations.

idioms.name: raw_c_arrays
idioms.description: C-style arrays decay to pointers and lose size information. Use std::array for fixed-size, std::vector for dynamic.

idioms.name: raw_c_strings
idioms.description: const char* parameters are error-prone (null, no size info). Use std::string_view for read-only, std::string for owned.
